title: podman logs command doesn't work
body:
**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**
`podman logs` command doesn't work

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. Create a new Jenkins container:
```
ab@ab-podman1:~> podman run -p 8080:8080 -p 50000:50000 --restart=on-failure -v jenkins_home:/var/jenkins_home
```

2. Here is the created container:
```
ab@ab-podman1:~> podman ps
CONTAINER ID  IMAGE                                COMMAND       CREATED         STATUS             PORTS                                             NAMES
[...]
a0331c1fe939  docker.io/jenkins/jenkins:lts-jdk11                37 seconds ago  Up 37 seconds ago  0.0.0.0:8080->8080/tcp, 0.0.0.0:50000->50000/tcp  kind_northcutt
```

**Describe the results you received:**
Try to check the created container logs:
```
ab@ab-podman1:~> podman logs kind_northcutt
Error: initial journal cursor: failed to get cursor: cannot assign requested address

ab@ab-podman1:~> podman logs a0331c1fe939
Error: initial journal cursor: failed to get cursor: cannot assign requested address
```

**Describe the results you expected:**
Container logs

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**
I See this issue for all created containers in my environment.

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
ab@ab-podman1:~> podman version
Version:      3.4.4
API Version:  3.4.4
Go Version:   go1.13.15
Built:        Tue Mar 29 12:00:00 2022
OS/Arch:      linux/amd64
```

**Output of `podman info --debug`:**


**Package info (e.g. output of `rpm -q podman` or `apt list podman`):**

```
ab@ab-podman1:~> rpm -q podman
podman-3.4.4-150400.2.14.x86_64
```

**Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):**
SUSE Linux Enterprise Server 15 SP4 working on Google Cloud Compute Engine

**Troubleshooting**

2. Based on https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/10987#issuecomment-884280903, my user is part of the `wheel` group and has the root privileges:
```
ab@ab-podman1:~> id
uid=1001(ab) gid=100(users) groups=100(users),474(docker),480(wheel),483(video),1000(google-sudoers)

ab@ab-podman1:~> sudo cat /etc/sudoers.d/google_sudoers
%google-sudoers ALL=(ALL:ALL) NOPASSWD:ALL
```

3. Check the `eventsLogger` and `logDriver` values:
```
ab@ab-podman1:~> podman info | grep -i log
  eventLogger: journald
  logDriver: journald
  log:
```

4. I am using the default `containers.conf` file located in the the `/usr/share/containers` directory
```
ab@ab-podman1:~> ls -lh /usr/share/containers/containers.conf
-rw-r--r-- 1 root root 15K May  7 21:21 /usr/share/containers/containers.conf`
```

5. The container has no IP:
```
ab@ab-podman1:~> podman inspect a0331c1fe939 | grep -i IP
            "IPAddress": "",
            "IPPrefixLen": 0,
            "IPv6Gateway": "",
            "GlobalIPv6Address": "",
            "GlobalIPv6PrefixLen": 0,
            "LinkLocalIPv6Address": "",
            "LinkLocalIPv6PrefixLen": 0,
                        "HostIp": "",
                        "HostIp": "",
                "org.opencontainers.image.description": "The Jenkins Continuous Integration and Delivery server",
                        "HostIp": "",
                        "HostIp": "",
            "IpcMode": "private",
```

comments.body:
Thanks for reaching out, @ab-mohamed!

Can you try with a more recent version of Podman?  If you want the issue fixed in SLES, please reach out via the SUSE customer channels as there is nothing we can do upstream.

Thanks, @vrothberg 

Kudos to @dcermak, you need to add the podman user to the `systemd-journal` group. 

```
ab@ab-podman1:~> sudo usermod -a -G systemd-journal ab

ab@ab-podman1:~> logout

ab@ab-podman1:~> podman logs 258e58487349
 _____________
< Hello-World >
 -------------
    \
     \
      \
                    ##        .
              ## ## ##       ==
           ## ## ## ##      ===
       /""""""""""""""""___/ ===
  ~~~ {~~ ~~~~ ~~~ ~~~~ ~~ ~ /  ===- ~~~
       \______ o          __/
        \    \        __/
          \____\______/
```

@vrothberg I am curious about one bit though, I have tried bisecting this issue and found out that podman 4.0 and later work flawlessly on Leap/SLE. Do they fallback to the file logger or do they employ some tricks to get access to the journal?

and a clarification for future googlers: the issue is that podman was logging to journald, but the user couldn't access the journal. You can check whether this is the case by running `journalctl` as root and looking for the container logs and trying the same as the ordinary (non-root) user. If you can't read the journal, then you can either give your user access or switch to a different logging mechanism.

>  Do they fallback to the file logger or do they employ some tricks to get access to the journal?

Yes, there were some changes (see https://github.com/containers/common/commit/09546d80af6d46fd1bb3f4ea120e16fed73ca12d) that addressed this very issue making Podman smarter in figuring out whether the user/Podman can access and use the journal.
@vrothberg would it be possible to cherry-pick that improvement into the `v3.4` maintenance branch? I think this is a pretty substantial improvement and would be worth backporting.

Upstream we recommend using more recent version of Podman.  For backports to that old versions, we need a Red Hat bugzilla.

A quick workaround could be to configure the file driver as the default on in containers.conf.  When will Podman be updated in SLES the next time?

> A quick workaround could be to configure the file driver as the default on in containers.conf.

Is this an official recommendation or a workaround as has been mentioned in the [past](https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/10987#issuecomment-884774628). If we were to suggest a potential solution to a user who's still experiencing this issue, would that be to add the non-root user running podman to `systemd-journal` group or updating containers.conf to use the `file` driver.
